summary: Patrick reports mid-month sales stats for May 2007, showing 15 total sales (10 downloads, 5 CDs) generating about $399 in gross revenue and $312 in profit. His recent focus on fixing usability bugs has noticeably boosted sales. CD sales are increasing but cut into margins since he subsidizes each one by about fifty cents. His AdWords campaign is performing exceptionally well at 22 cents per trial download, well below his 30-cent target, making customer acquisition highly profitable.
key_points: Fixing usability bugs directly translated into higher sales, demonstrating the ROI of polish over new features
AdWords cost per trial download dropped to $0.22, meaning each $24.95 sale costs only about $7.30 to acquire — a roughly 3.4x return
CD sales are growing but slightly reduce profit margins due to per-unit subsidization of about fifty cents
Monthly expenses are modest at roughly $88, keeping the business highly profitable relative to revenue
money_quotes: Sales have been nicely juiced by my crusade against usability bugs these last two weeks.
At $.22 it costs me about $7.30 to buy a $24.95 sale. As you might expect, I'm happy to do that all day long.
I'm trying to tweak the campaign to increase number of clicks now because at $.22 it costs me about $7.30 to buy a $24.95 sale.
Higher CD sales will likely lead to higher absolute sales numbers and slightly lower profits since I subsidize every one by about fifty cents relative to downloads.
key_insight: When your customer acquisition cost is less than a third of your sale price, the rational move is to scale spending aggressively rather than optimize further.
